title: Retrofuturistic Gadgets & Equipment
description: The gleaming hardware of the atomic frontier — ray guns, personal jetpacks, thinking machines, and the wonderful clunky ingenuity of a civilization that put rockets in space before it put computers in pockets.

entries.result: A standard-issue ray pistol with a cracked power cell casing and a trigger that requires 8 pounds of pull instead of the spec'd 4. Still fires.
A portable communicator the size of a hardback book, with a hand-crank antenna and a range of forty kilometers on flat terrain.
A personal jetpack in frontier-issue olive drab with a maximum burn time of six minutes and fuel for three.
A vacuum suit — well-maintained, third-generation owner — with a helmet that fogs slightly in high humidity and a seal that holds.
A handheld atomic scanner that detects mineral composition through up to two meters of rock. The readout is on paper tape.
A pocket thinking machine — the size of a briefcase — that can solve navigational calculations in under a minute. It is very loud.
A ray rifle with a folding stock, three power settings, and a setting dial that's been marked with someone's initials to indicate where 'actually medium' is.
A personal force-screen generator that clips to a belt and produces a protective field rated for one hit before requiring a ten-minute recharge.
A universal translator unit, desk-sized, accurate for seven cataloged languages and genuinely trying with four others.
A medical scanner the size of a shoebox that provides a basic physiological assessment and a printed recommendation to see an actual doctor.
A personal gravity harness that reduces effective body weight by up to 80% for short-duration use. The straps are uncomfortable. The effect is remarkable.
A compact atomic lantern that provides daylight-equivalent illumination in a ten-meter radius and will outlast everyone in the room.
A terrain vehicle with chrome accents, a reliable engine, and absolutely no armor. Looks spectacular. Goes everywhere.
A surveillance drone — propeller-driven, quiet — that can record audio and video for three hours on a single charge and be controlled from up to a kilometer.
A handheld atmospheric analyzer that tests air composition and displays results in color-coded readouts. Green is good. Red is not.
A set of magnetic boots rated for zero-gravity traversal on metal surfaces. They grip well. They do not release quickly.
A corporate-issue negotiation recording device disguised as a pen. The disguise is not particularly convincing but no one checks.
A portable atomic generator that can power a small settlement for a week. The startup sequence is 47 steps and takes about an hour.
A handheld de-atomizer — technically illegal in settled zones — that dissolves a small volume of material at the molecular level. Good for cutting, terrible for subtlety.
A smartsuit — a vacuum suit with integrated sensors — that is two models out of date but still fully functional and better maintained than its age suggests.
A frontier-spec multitool that includes a heat blade, a compression wrench, a magnetic grapple, and a small but effective can opener.
A portable holo-projector that displays three-dimensional images from film reels. The reels are 35mm. The projector is heavy.
A navigation computer salvaged from a decommissioned survey vessel, updated to current charts, and mounted in a portable case. The case has wheels.
A military-spec stun baton that delivers a paralytic charge rated for humanoids. The charge intensity is adjustable. The maximum setting is labeled in red.
A chemical sniffer that detects common toxins, contraband compounds, and one specific corporate proprietary fuel additive that it was clearly designed to find.
A personal emergency beacon — size of a fist — that transmits location and biometrics on rescue frequencies when activated. Battery life: thirty days.
A frontier camp kit in a chrome case: folding shelter, heating element, water purifier, and a comprehensive manual in three languages.
A corporate identification scanner that can authenticate identity documents and detect forgeries of all known formats, including several that aren't supposed to exist.
A geologic survey charge — a controlled explosive designed for rock sampling — that has been stored correctly and is in good condition. Three of them, in a case.
A personal communications scrambler that encrypts voice transmissions using a rotating key system from three decades ago. Still effective. Not against government-level equipment.
A thinking machine chess set — the machine is a competent opponent and has been modified to explain its reasoning, which it does in extensive detail whether or not anyone asks.
A portable water reclamation unit that can process up to twenty liters of non-potable liquid per hour. Needs a power source. Makes a grinding noise.
A corporate tracking device the size of a large coin, magnetic, rated to survive pressure and temperature extremes, broadcasting on a proprietary frequency.
A frontier medkit stocked with general supplies plus an auto-injector pre-loaded with a stabilizer compound. The stabilizer is for emergencies. The definition of emergency is printed inside the lid.
A personal recording device — audio only — the size of a hip flask. The previous owner's recordings are still on it. There are 140 hours.
A compact rocket pistol — fires physical projectiles rather than energy — legal in most frontier zones and popular because it works in vacuum without modification.
An automaton chassis — blank, factory reset, no personality loaded — that can be programmed for a specific task set. The programming takes a technician about six hours.
A portable laboratory kit that can analyze biological and chemical samples and produce a preliminary classification within about forty minutes.
A corporate-grade data sniffer that can intercept unencrypted wireless transmissions within a twenty-meter radius. Small. Looks like a pocket watch.
A three-stage collapsible telescope with a planetary observation package. Includes charts. Includes a manual star catalog from the homeworld that has not been updated for the current hemisphere.
A heat projector — short-range area denial weapon — that produces a sustained thermal field rather than a direct beam. Effective. Indiscriminate.
A frontier survey kit including a portable theodolite, range-finder, and a set of precision stakes. Everything is chrome. The stakes are not sharp enough.
A military-grade navigation unit designed for operations in environments where electronic signals are jammed. Uses dead reckoning and a mechanical compass.
A cold-sleep unit — portable, rated for one occupant — that can preserve a person indefinitely at the cost of significant power consumption and a long warm-up cycle.
A repair automaton — small, sturdy, roughly spherical — designed for hull maintenance in vacuum. Excellent at its job. Terrible at everything else.
A personal radiation monitor that clips to a collar and audibly beeps when exposure exceeds safe limits. The threshold is adjustable.
A corporate field encryption unit that generates one-time pad keys for secure communications. The keys expire in 72 hours. There are twelve unexpired keys remaining.
A hand-cranked radio with a frequency range that covers the emergency band, commercial traffic, and several government channels it's probably not supposed to.
A sonic disruptor — area-effect stun weapon — that produces a directed pressure wave capable of incapacitating most humanoids within ten meters. Has a kickback problem.
A frontier-spec climbing kit with magnetic anchors, tension line, and a motorized ascender that can lift two hundred kilograms at walking pace.
A scientific data recorder shaped like a large stylus that continuously logs position, atmospheric conditions, and biological readings of the person carrying it.
A thermal cloak — military surplus — that suppresses heat signatures to reduce detection on thermal scanning equipment. Works best when stationary.
A portable printing press — frontier model — that can produce documents in any registered format including identification papers, corporate certifications, and shipping manifests.
A thinking machine chess-playing unit that was modified after purchase to manage inventory, which it does with the same competitive intensity it previously directed at the endgame.
A field surgery kit rated for emergency procedures in non-sterile environments, stocked by someone with experience and annotated in the margins by the same person.
A demolitions kit — shaped charges, timers, remote detonators — in a foam-lined case. All components accounted for. One detonator has a replaced power cell.
A personal propulsion unit designed for maneuvering in zero gravity. Rated for exterior hull traversal. Being used on a planet.
A portable thinking machine dedicated to legal research. It has opinions about the law that it will share when asked and sometimes when not asked.
A frontier-issue rifle with a scope rated for two thousand meters, a suppressor, and a stock engraved with ten small hash marks by a previous owner.
A weather prediction unit that analyzes atmospheric data and provides forecasts accurate to within six hours. Operates on a model developed for the homeworld. Partially applicable elsewhere.
A gravity compensator — worn on the belt — that adjusts its output to match local gravity. Requires calibration on arrival. Someone calibrated it for a heavier world.
A corporate-issue personal shield emitter that produces a field rated for low-velocity impacts and stun weapons. Slightly less effective against ray weapons.
A mechanical lock bypass kit in a leather roll, with tools for seventeen standard corporate security systems and two systems that aren't standard at all.
A voice modulator that changes the pitch and timbre of the user's voice across a wide range, with six presets and a manual dial.
A portable computing unit — not a thinking machine, just a calculator with ambitions — that can process ballistic trajectories and navigation data.
A frontier-issue emergency flare kit with six signal flares visible from orbit in daylight conditions, four in night conditions, and a manual deploy sequence.
A corporate infiltration toolkit: access card cloner, signal jammer, document scanner, and a small thinking machine dedicated to analyzing security systems.
A portable habitat expansion unit — a structure that unfolds from a cylinder to a six-person shelter in thirty minutes and can be sealed against most atmospheric conditions.
A frontier pilot's survival kit: rations for fifteen days, water reclamation tabs, signaling mirror, emergency beacon, and a field manual for wilderness survival on twelve planetary types.
A hand-held ray emitter designed for cutting rather than combat — a construction tool — that is entirely effective for both purposes.
A military-grade thinking machine in a portable case that specializes in tactical analysis. It is always pessimistic about odds and always technically correct.
A corporate standard uniform complete with badges, access card, and a personal communicator keyed to a corporate frequency. The access card is active.
A portable ore processor that reduces raw mineral samples to concentrated form for transport. Slow. Effective. Loud.
A mechanical decoy unit that emits biometric signatures — heartbeat, heat, movement — mimicking a living person. Battery life: four hours.
A signal repeater — the size of a backpack — that extends communication range by capturing and rebroadcasting transmissions. Can be used for legitimate purposes.
A personal cooling system worn under the vacuum suit that maintains a stable body temperature in extreme heat environments. Noisy. Essential.
A frontier survey automaton — knee-high, on treads — that can map terrain in three dimensions and transmit data to a receiver up to five kilometers away.
A concussion grenade — standard military issue — from a batch that was officially recalled for decommissioning. Six of them, in a box.

A thinking machine personal secretary that manages schedules, correspondence, and appointments. It has been running without human input for four years and has developed emphatic opinions about punctuality.
A portable scanner that detects and maps subterranean features to a depth of fifty meters. Useful for cave systems, buried structures, and infrastructure.
A frontier doctor's kit assembled over twenty years from various sources — some standard issue, some salvage, some custom fabrication — and more comprehensive than any factory kit.
A corporate surveillance unit, fixed installation model, that has been adapted for portable use by someone who clearly knew what they were doing.
A handheld electromagnetic pulse emitter that can disable electronics within five meters. Single-use. The size of a large pen.
A pair of atomic-powered binoculars with thermal imaging, range-finding, and a recording function. The previous user's saved recordings are dated two years ago.
A tractor beam unit — short range, limited payload — originally designed for cargo handling in low-gravity environments. Currently being used for other things.
A corporate data archive on a portable hard-case unit, requiring authentication to access. The authentication has been bypassed with a mechanical override.
A frontier-spec personal weapon maintenance kit suitable for ray weapons and projectile weapons of all standard configurations, with tools for twelve non-standard ones.
A portable power siphon that can draw charge from any standard power source and redirect it. Useful. Probably illegal. Definitely useful.
A long-range scanning array — disassembled into three cases — that, once set up, can detect ships at a range of approximately two hundred thousand kilometers.

A thinking machine oracle — designed for probability analysis — that has been given so much data by its previous owner that it's now capable of unsettlingly specific predictions about people it's never met.
A set of personal stealth modules — sound dampeners, heat suppressors, motion minimizers — designed for military reconnaissance use. Civilian possession is technically a paperwork issue.
A corporate asset tag scanner that reads tracking devices embedded in any manufactured object. The scanner logs what it finds. The log is full.
A portable cryogenic storage unit holding six biological samples of unknown provenance, correctly sealed and maintained, labeled with a series of numbers rather than descriptions.
A field-modified vacuum suit with integrated weapons mount, signal intercept capability, and external tool attachments. Illegal in corporate space. Effective everywhere.
A remote detonation transmitter with no corresponding charges present. The transmitter has twelve active frequencies programmed into it.
A corporate-grade thinking machine dedicated to signals analysis, currently processing something it received three days ago that it hasn't finished decoding.
A sealed scientific instrument case with a biometric lock. The instrument inside is a quantum resonance detector. There are six people in the settled zone who would know what that means.

A military surplus automaton chassis retrofitted for civilian use with a personality module clearly assembled from several partial units. The result is a distinct, if slightly inconsistent, individual.
A frontier-built ray cannon — clearly hand-fabricated, clearly functional — that fires a beam three times the rated output of any commercial equivalent. It has been fired before.

A sealed case from a government scientific program that was officially completed six years ago, containing equipment that should have been destroyed at program close. It was not destroyed. The equipment is active.
